perturbation.original_text: Watchit agrees to provide Oceanic Time Warner Cable the following content: - Watchit Gaming Guides - Lunchtime with Ira segments every week Watchit will provide the content on BetaSp format and reserves the right to modify the content to reflect sponsorship by an advertiser and advertisers.
perturbation.changed_text: Watchit agrees to provide Oceanic Time Warner Cable the following content: - Watchit Gaming Guides - Lunchtime with Ira segments every week Watchit will provide the programming on BetaSp format and maintains the exclusive right to alter the programming content to integrate sponsorships from advertisers and various promotional partners.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'content' to 'programming' introduces ambiguity. While seemingly synonymous, legal context might differentiate them. Specifically, changing 'modify the content to reflect sponsorship by an advertiser and advertisers' to 'alter the programming content to integrate sponsorships from advertisers and various promotional partners' introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text might imply minor adjustments to existing content, while the changed text suggests a broader right to reshape the programming itself. This contradiction could affect how 'Oceanic Time Warner Cable' interprets its rights, especially concerning editing. This introduces confusion to the contract, creating legal ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Oceanic Time Warner Cable agrees to not edit or modify the above content.
perturbation.justification: The change from "modify the content to reflect sponsorship" to "alter the programming content to integrate sponsorships" expands Watchit's rights to modify the material. This directly contradicts Oceanic Time Warner Cable's agreement that they "agree to not edit or modify the above content." The original text implied a more limited scope of modification (e.g., adding a sponsor's logo), while the changed text implies a broader right to reshape the programming. This broader right directly clashes with Oceanic Time Warner Cable's right to receive unmodified content, thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable will provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the content provided by Watchit.
perturbation.changed_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable will provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the broadcasts provided by Watchit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'content' to 'broadcasts' creates a conflict because the agreement stipulates that Oceanic Time Warner Cable must include a specific disclaimer on the Watchit 'Content'. If 'content' and 'broadcasts' are interpreted differently, the obligation to include the disclaimer on all 'broadcasts' becomes unclear. Is the disclaimer required for all broadcasts, or only those that are considered 'content' as originally defined? 'Content' may be a more general description of any material provided, while 'broadcasts' implies a structured transmission. This change causes uncertainty over what specifically requires the disclaimer and generates potential legal challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Oceanic Time Warner Cable will include the following disclaimer on the Watchit Content: "The materials shown are for entertainment purposes only and does not provide any guarantees of winning or improving your odds of winning on this program. The rules of the games as shown may not apply to all properties and may change from time to time."
perturbation.justification: The change from 'content' to 'broadcasts' creates a contradiction because the original agreement specifies a disclaimer on 'Watchit Content'. If 'content' and 'broadcasts' are not equivalent, the obligation to include the disclaimer becomes ambiguous. The modified text introduces uncertainty regarding whether the disclaimer applies to all broadcasts or only those also considered 'content'. This ambiguity directly contradicts the section requiring the disclaimer, rendering enforcement unclear and creating a potential legal challenge. As stated in the Perturbation Explanation, 'Content' may be a more general description of any material provided, while 'broadcasts' implies a structured transmission. This change causes uncertainty over what specifically requires the disclaimer and generates potential legal challenges.

perturbation.original_text: Watchit shall have the exclusive right to sell third party advertising as sponsors of their content and will have the right to brand the content under the Watchit brand and place a "bug" on the screen identifying the content with a Watchit trademark.
perturbation.changed_text: Watchit shall have the exclusive right to sell third party advertising as sponsors of their program and will have the right to brand the program under the Watchit brand and place a "bug" on the screen identifying the content with a Watchit trademark.
perturbation.explanation: Switching from 'content' to 'program' creates an issue because the right to place a "bug" (trademark) is specified for 'content'. Since 'program' is now used for advertising and branding rights, it implies that the "bug" only applies when the program itself is branded, potentially excluding cases where individual content segments within the program should also display the trademark. The original text granted Watchit the right to trademark specific segments; this right is now ambiguous. Because it still mentions 'content' in the bug definition, there is ambiguity as to which the 'bug' applies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Watchit shall have the exclusive right to sell third party advertising as sponsors of their program and will have the right to brand the program under the Watchit brand and place a "bug" on the screen identifying the content with a Watchit trademark.
perturbation.justification: The changed text and the contradicted text are identical. While the Perturbation Explanation argues that changing 'content' to 'program' could introduce ambiguity regarding the application of the 'bug' trademark, the fact that the contradicted text is the same means the change does not create a strong in-text contradiction. The contradictory text still uses the original 'content' in reference to the 'bug'.
